blocks.statement: I can apply ΔU = Q − W to gas systems, assign correct signs to Q (heat in = +) and W (work BY system = +), and predict ΔU in heating, cooling, expansion, and compression cases.

blocks.whatWeKnow: After impact, the fireball is a high-temperature gas EXPANDING against the surrounding atmosphere — a thermodynamic system.
blocks.connection: The first law describes how the fireball's internal energy converts to WORK pushing on the surrounding air — that's the shockwave. Day 11 will partition the impact KE using this framework.

blocks.markdown:
## Energy bookkeeping for a gas

**ΔU = Q − W** — the first law of thermodynamics. It's conservation of energy, written for gases: the change in a system's internal energy equals the heat that flows IN minus the work the system does on its surroundings.

There are exactly two ways to change a gas's internal energy U:

1. **Heat (Q)** crosses the boundary. Heat IN: Q > 0. Heat OUT: Q < 0.
2. **Work (W)** is done across the boundary. The gas EXPANDS and pushes on the surroundings: W > 0 (the system spends energy). The gas is COMPRESSED by an outside push: W < 0 (the system gains energy).

A bike pump warms up with NO flame anywhere — you do work ON the gas, so W is negative, and ΔU = 0 − (−W) is positive. Compression alone raises temperature. The impact fireball runs the same equation in reverse: a huge ΔU drives expansion work on the air — the shockwave.

terms.definition: The total energy contained inside a system — sum of all molecules' KE and bond PE. Symbol: U. Units: Joules. Heating a gas raises U. Cooling lowers U. Compressing it without heat exchange ALSO raises U (work done on the system).
terms.cognate: Sp. energía interna · Pt. energia interna · HC enèji entèn

terms.definition: Change in internal energy = heat added to the system MINUS work done BY the system on the surroundings. Energy conservation, written for gases. Example: 500 J of heat IN, gas does 200 J of work expanding → ΔU = 500 − 200 = 300 J. Internal energy rises by 300 J.
terms.cognate: Sp. primera ley · Pt. primeira lei

blocks.title: U lives INSIDE — Q and W cross the boundary
blocks.markdown: U is the system's OWN energy. Q and W are how energy MOVES across the boundary. ΔU = what stays. Don't write Q or W as 'energy the gas has' — they're flows, not contents.

blocks.instruction: Draw a gas in a cylinder with a movable piston. Label Q (heat crossing the boundary), W (work at the piston), and ΔU (inside the gas). Then sketch the signs for the three cases.
prompts: Case 1: heat enters and the gas expands — signs of Q, W, ΔU?
Case 2: heat enters and the gas is held still — signs?
Case 3: a bike pump compresses the gas with no heat exchange — signs?

blocks.prompt: HEAT A GAS, IT EXPANDS — 500 J of heat is added to a gas. The gas does 200 J of work as it expands. What is ΔU?
blocks.given: Q = +500 J (heat IN) · W = +200 J (work BY system, expansion)
blocks.answer: Internal energy rises by 300 J. Some heat became work; the rest stayed.

blocks.prompt: BIKE PUMP (ADIABATIC COMPRESSION) — You compress air in a bike pump. 300 J of work is done ON the gas (so W = −300 J from the system's perspective). No heat is exchanged (adiabatic; Q = 0). What is ΔU? Why does the pump get warm?
blocks.given: Q = 0 (adiabatic — no heat in) · W = −300 J (work done ON the gas)
blocks.answer: Internal energy rises by 300 J → temperature rises. That's why the pump is warm.

blocks.title: SIGNS, SIGNS, SIGNS
blocks.markdown: Stop on every problem and ask: is heat going IN or OUT? Is work being done BY the system or ON it? Get these right and the math is easy.

blocks.prompt: SYSTEM DOES MORE WORK THAN HEAT ADDED — 100 J of heat enters a system. The system does 150 J of work on the surroundings. What is ΔU? What's happening physically?
blocks.givenHint: Q = +100 J (heat IN) · W = +150 J (work BY the system)
blocks.equationHint: ΔU = Q − W — the system does MORE work than the heat coming in, so watch the sign of ΔU.

blocks.prompt: Check your YOUR TURN answer: 100 J of heat enters a system and the system does 150 J of work. What is ΔU, and what does the sign tell you? Then complete the bike-pump frame.
blocks.frame: When the bike pump warms up, the work I do ON the gas becomes ___. With Q = 0 and W negative (work done ON the gas), ΔU is ___ — so T ___.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **24.1–24.3** (absolute zero, the first law of thermodynamics, and adiabatic processes). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
